item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it would be possible to crouch in morse code. And the Foundation could communicate Xblananque through the method of teabagging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really creative, taking haunted gaming to the next level

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending freaks me out. I love this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes me happy for reasons I can't fully explain. It's a great mix of retro nostalgia (and someone who actually remembers the Panther prototype) with a fresh and interesting take on a culture not often explored in the modern world. It even inspired some curiousity in me to do some research on my own about these Mayan gods.

+1 for a great SCP

item.title: Re: Reserved
item.content:encoded:
Leonard says all the scans of his manuals are covered by fair use and can be used with attribution.

If that’s not sufficient for SCP-JP the author of E.T the Earthen Totality would probably be willing to make fresh pixel art to replace them.

item.title: Re: Reserved
item.content:encoded:
Any non-cc images used with permission of Leonard Herman

Could you elaborate about this? While I'm interested in translating Atari Arcadia articles, what this statement means confuses me.

Did Leonard Herman permitted to use the images under CC BY-SA 3.0, or only in SCP wiki articles exceptionally? If the latter, unfortunately, Japanese Branch moderators would not permit us to use these in translated version.

item.title: Re: Reserved
item.content:encoded:
Any non-cc images used with permission of Leonard Herman

This is a great ride and I feel really develops the little universe Arcadia is building for itself. I’ve always enjoyed the Panther as a forgotten bit of history and I like seeing it as an SCP.

item.title: Reserved
item.content:encoded:
I told you I'd attribute them!

The Sketch of the Panther is taken from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Atari_Panther_chassis.png Under CC 3.0

The Atari mindlink image is used with permission of Mr. Herman, as Roget said.

The image of "Walter Green" is from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ian_Grojnowski,_circa_mid-1990s_(re-scanned;_headshot).jpg Image created by George M. Bergman, under CC 4.0
